what_it_does: Design note that reframes the BD-G overlay as a voice-command surface, proposing that the V control acts as both a radial-menu opener on tap and a push-to-talk microphone trigger on press-and-hold, while the overlay displays context-specific valid voice commands and speakable radial labels.
observations: The V control has two roles: a tap opens the radial command center; press-and-hold is push-to-talk and release ends capture.
Contextual voice-command grammar is shown on the overlay so the user does not have to memorize commands.
Each radial slice has a spoken label, allowing voice selection of radial actions.
Voice mode-switching is emphasized so the user can switch modes while pointing at an element.
The note cites a ResearchGate paper titled "What can I say?" as background on VUI discoverability failure.
The intended user is explicitly not a developer and cannot be given a command list to memorize.
standouts: Press-and-hold V as push-to-talk to address accidental activation and privacy concerns.
Overlay shows only currently valid voice commands for the selected target.
Radial slices are speakable labels, enabling hands-busy mode switching.
conventions: Uses YAML front matter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, and history.
Markdown body mirrors the title as a section heading.
Presents design rationale as bullet points with bold lead phrases.
References an external research paper informally without URL or citation details.
concerns: No concrete command grammar, recognition implementation, or error handling is specified.
Assumes the V control can distinguish tap from press-and-hold at the hardware/input level.
ResearchGate citation is vague and not verifiable from this file alone.
Persona claim ("he is not a developer") is stated without supporting user-research evidence.
notes: A design rationale note arguing the V overlay should combine radial menu access with push-to-talk voice control and live command hints.
questions: What exactly is the BD-G overlay and its current visual/interaction state?
Which hardware or input layer implements the tap vs. press-and-hold V behavior?
What is the complete set of radial slices and their spoken labels?
How is the contextual voice grammar generated and updated as selection changes?
What happens if the user releases V before a command is recognized or completed?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the title says the overlay is reframed as a voice-command surface, and the body explains the PTT, contextual grammar, and speakable radial labels that realize that reframing.
intention_signals: To make voice control feel safe, discoverable, and hands-compatible for a non-developer user by binding speech capture to a deliberate physical hold and displaying only valid commands for the current context.
novelty: Collapses the radial overlay, push-to-talk microphone, and dynamic command vocabulary display into a single thumb-reachable control.
contribution: The dual-use V control concept and the principle of showing context-sensitive voice grammar directly on the overlay so users do not need to memorize commands.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design note that reframes the BD-G overlay's V control as a press-and-hold push-to-talk surface with a radial, contextually labeled voice-command grammar for non-developer users.
intention_for_embedding: To specify a voice-first overlay interaction where tapping V opens a radial menu and holding V captures speech, with visible command hints for discoverability and hands-busy mode switching.
